questions: how to push code to github
answers: Git is a version control system - it tracks changes in your code so you can go back, collaborate, and manage projects. Every developer must learn it!
Essential git commands: git init, git add, git commit, git push, git pull, git branch, git merge. These 7 commands handle 90% of daily work.
GitHub workflow: create repo, clone it, make changes, git add, git commit, git push. For collaboration: fork, branch, pull request, merge.
Git tip: commit often with clear messages, use branches for features, never push directly to main, and learn git log and git diff for debugging.

feelings.question: git and github ki?
feelings.answer: Yes, Git and GitHub are both commonly used version control systems that allow developers to manage their code changes collaboratively. The question is asking about the basics of using these tools for project management.

feelings.question: how to use git branch?
feelings.answer: Git branches are used for managing separate development or testing versions of your codebase. To create a new branch, you can use the `git checkout` command followed by the name of the branch you want to create (e.g., `git checkout -b my-new-branch`). Once you've created a branch, you can start working on it and commit changes to that branch using the same `git push` command. If you need to revert back to an earlier version of your codebase, you can use the `git reset --hard` command to undo any changes made since the last commit.

feelings.question: github pull request kivabe kore?
feelings.answer: GitHub pull requests (PRs) are used to propose changes or improvements to a project's codebase. To create and submit a PR, you need to have an account on GitHub and fork the relevant repository. Once you have made your changes, make sure to follow the guidelines for submitting a PR, such as adding comments explaining your changes and including tests where applicable.
